content: Let's cut through the documentation noise and look at what actually happens under the hood when you write #[my_attr] on a function or struct. The docs tell you that an attribute macro is a proc-macro triggered by the compiler, but they gloss over the messy reality of how the compiler passes data to your macro system and expects it back. At its core, this mechanism is about parsing arbitrary syntax strings into structured Rust types using syn, then transforming those structures before returning them to the compiler as TokenStream. When you see #[my_attr] on a function or struct in your source file, the compiler has already performed lexical analysis, converting that text into an internal representation it can pass to your proc-macro implementation. That internal representation isn't just raw tokens; it's a parsed tree structure containing information about the attribute arguments and the item being decorated. Your macro receives this as an Attribute object from syn, which holds the attribute name and its arguments in a structured format like Vec<String> or Enum variants depending on what your parser generated. Inside your proc-macro implementation, you take that Attribute input and parse it further using syn to extract the specific tokens you care about—maybe extracting function names, struct field names, or configuration parameters from #[my_attr(...)] syntax. This parsing step is where you build a TokenStream representing the parsed attribute arguments, often using helper methods like syn::parse_str or custom parsers tailored to your macro's grammar constraints. Once you have that structured argument representation, it becomes easy to manipulate because you're working with concrete Rust types rather than raw token sequences. You can then use this structure to generate new code by constructing fresh TokenStream objects representing the transformed item—whether that means adding logging statements before a function call or conditionally removing fields from a struct definition. The return value of your proc-macro must be a TokenStream, which represents the output tokens that the compiler will concatenate with the rest of the file and re-parse. This entire flow—from receiving the parsed attribute data to constructing the new token stream—is where the real magic lives. You aren't just decorating code; you're intercepting the compilation pipeline at the syntax tree level, allowing your macro to understand context-specific details like function names or struct fields that would be impossible to capture with simple string interpolation. This is why we prefer parsing arguments into syn types rather than relying on string formatting; it gives us type safety and predictable behavior when dealing with complex attribute syntax involving multiple parameters or nested structures. The compiler doesn't care about the exact token sequence you return as long as it's valid Rust code that matches the original item's signature, which means your macro can safely reorder attributes, inject conditional logic, or completely rewrite portions of the source file while maintaining compatibility with downstream compilation steps.
